'''目'''（さかん）
#日本の[[律令制]][[四等官]]のうち四等官を指す。「目」の文字は[[国司]]の四等官（中央政府における「[[主典]]」に相当する）を指す。主に、官事の記録及び公文の草案作成等を掌った。特に[[大国]]と呼ばれる最上級の[[令制国]]には特に[[大目]]・[[少目]]が設置された。 [[Category:律令制の官制|さかん]]

== 概要 == 「目」の文字は[[国司]]の四等官（中央政府における「主典」に相当する）を指す。主に官事の記録及び公文の草案作成等を掌った。特に[[大国 (令制国)|大国]]と呼ばれる最上級の[[令制国]]には特に'''大目'''・'''少目'''が設置された。

revision.comment: Remove 0x200e ∵Check Wikipedia ID16
revision.text:
'''目'''（さかん）とは、日本の[[律令制]][[四等官]]のうち四等官を指す。
== 概要 == 「目」の文字は[[国司]]の四等官（中央政府における「主典」に相当する）を指す。主に官事の記録及び公文の草案作成等を掌った。特に[[大国 (令制国)|大国]]と呼ばれる最上級の[[令制国]]には特に'''大目'''・'''少目'''が設置された。
== 関連項目 == *[[目代]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:さかん}} [[Category:律令制の国司|*さかん]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''目'''（さかん）とは、日本の[[律令制]][[四等官]]のうち四等官を指す。 == 概要 == 「目」の文字は[[国司]]の四等官（中央政府における「主典」に相当する）を指す。主に官事の記録及び公文の草案作成等を掌った。特に[[大国 (令制国)|大国]]と呼ばれる最上級の[[令制国]]には特に'''大目'''・'''少目'''が設置された。 == 刀匠や浄瑠璃における掾号 == 律令国司における目が転じて、後世、[[朝廷]]から、出入の[[商人]]や[[刀匠]]、[[浄瑠璃]]の芸人などに対して、その技芸を顕彰する意味で下賜された官名安田富美子「近世受領考」『古浄瑠璃正本』（角川書店、1967年）など。有名なのは寛文3年（[[1663年]]）に正六位下本来は、律令下で上国の主典は、従八位下[[越後目]]（えちごのさかん）を受領した藤原貞勝（のちの都万大夫「大夫」は本来、五位の称号）や、従六位下[[佐渡目]]（さどのさかん）を受領した佐渡嶋雅好が知られている『浄瑠璃太夫口宣案』、『烏丸家記五諸人上卿之留』。 == 脚注 ==
== 関連項目 == *[[目代]] {{DEFAULTSORT:さかん}} [[Category:律令制の国司|*さかん]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''目'''（さかん）とは、日本の[[律令制]][[四等官]]のうち四等官を指す。 == 概要 == 「目」の文字は[[国司]]の四等官（中央政府における「主典」に相当する）を指す。主に官事の記録及び公文の草案作成等を掌った。特に[[大国 (令制国)|大国]]と呼ばれる最上級の[[令制国]]には特に'''大目'''・'''少目'''が設置された。 == 刀匠や浄瑠璃における目号（または大目・少目） == 律令国司における目（大国では大目・少目）が転じて、後世、[[朝廷]]から、出入の[[商人]]や[[刀匠]]、[[浄瑠璃]]の芸人などに対して、その技芸を顕彰する意味で下賜された官名安田富美子「近世受領考」『古浄瑠璃正本』（角川書店、1967年）など。有名なのは寛文3年（[[1663年]]）に正六位下本来は、律令下で上国の主典は、従八位下[[越後目]]（えちごのさかん）を受領した藤原貞勝（のちの都万大夫「大夫」は本来、五位の称号）や、従六位下[[佐渡目]]（さどのさかん）を受領した佐渡雅好（のちの佐渡嶋正吉）『浄瑠璃太夫口宣案』、『烏丸家記五諸人上卿之留』。 == 脚注 ==
== 関連項目 == *[[目代]] {{DEFAULTSORT:さかん}} [[Category:律令制の国司|*さかん]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''目'''（さかん）とは、日本の[[律令制]][[四等官]]のうち四等官を指す。 == 概要 == 「目」の文字は[[国司]]の四等官（中央政府における「主典」に相当する）を指す。主に官事の記録及び公文の草案作成等を掌った。特に[[大国 (令制国)|大国]]と呼ばれる最上級の[[令制国]]には特に'''大目'''・'''少目'''が設置された。 == 刀匠や浄瑠璃における目号（または大目・少目） == 律令国司における目（大国では大目・少目）が転じて、後世、[[朝廷]]から、出入の[[商人]]や[[刀匠]]、[[浄瑠璃]]の芸人などに対して、その技芸を顕彰する意味で下賜された官名安田富美子「近世受領考」『古浄瑠璃正本』（角川書店、1967年）など。有名なのは寛文3年（[[1663年]]）に正六位下本来は、律令下で上国の主典は、従八位下[[越後目]]（えちごのさかん）を受領本来、律令制で国司の三等官（判官）は任用（にんよう）と称す。幕府主導による武家官位が主流になった江戸時代以降は、朝廷寄りの文書でも混用が見られる。した藤原貞勝（のちの都万大夫「大夫」は本来、五位の称号）や、従六位下江戸時代の武家官位の下限は、大名が従五位下の諸大夫、旗本が正六位下の布衣、平士でも正七位下である。八位以下の受領は稀となった。[[佐渡目]]（さどのさかん）を受領した佐渡雅好（のちの佐渡嶋正吉）『浄瑠璃太夫口宣案』、『烏丸家記五諸人上卿之留』。 == 脚注 ==
== 関連項目 == *[[目代]] {{DEFAULTSORT:さかん}} [[Category:律令制の国司|*さかん]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''目'''（さかん）とは、[[日本]]の[[律令制]]下の[[四等官]]制において、[[国司]]の四等官（中央政府における「主典」に相当する）を指す。
中世以後、職人・芸人に宮中・宮家から名誉称号として授けられるようになった。
== 国司四等官の目 == 国司の四等官は、守（かみ）・介（すけ）・[[掾]]（じょう）・目（さかん）という文字を用いた。目は主に官事の記録及び公文の草案作成等を掌った。
大宝令・養老令に定められた規則では、[[令制国|国]]の規模（国力）によって国司の制度が異なっており、[[中国 (令制国)|中国]]や[[下国]]では介や掾が省かれたが、'''目'''は設置された。逆に、最も高い位置づけの[[大国 (令制国)|大国]]には'''大目'''・'''少目'''が設置された。
ただし実際の運用上は人員の増減があり、規則上は目が配置される上国に大目と少目が置かれることもあった（[[国司#国等級区分]]参照）。
== 職人・芸人の名誉称号 == 律令国司における目（大国では大目・少目）が転じて、後世、[[朝廷]]から、出入の[[商人]]や[[刀匠]]、[[浄瑠璃]]の芸人などに対して、その技芸を顕彰する意味で下賜されるようになった<ref>安田富美子「近世受領考」『古浄瑠璃正本』（角川書店、1967年）など</ref>。
[[浄瑠璃]]太夫に対する有名な事例としては、寛文3年（[[1663年]]）に正六位下{{efn|本来は、律令下で上国の主典は、従八位下。}}越後目（えちごのさかん）を受領{{efn|本来、律令制で国司の三等官（判官）は任用（にんよう）と称す。幕府主導による[[武家官位]]が主流になった江戸時代以降は、朝廷寄りの文書でも混用が見られる。}}した藤原貞勝（のちの都万大夫{{efn|「大夫」は本来、五位の称号}}）や、従六位下{{efn|江戸時代の武家官位の下限は、大名が従五位下の諸大夫、旗本が正六位下の布衣、平士でも正七位下である。八位以下の受領は稀となった。}}佐渡目（さどのさかん）を受領した佐渡雅好（のちの佐渡嶋正吉）<ref>『浄瑠璃太夫口宣案』、『烏丸家記五諸人上卿之留』</ref>が挙げられる。
== 脚注 == === 注釈 === {{notelist}} === 出典 === {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[目代]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:さかん}} [[Category:律令制の国司|*さかん]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''目'''（さかん）とは、[[日本]]の[[律令制]]下の[[四等官]]制において、[[国司]]の四等官（中央政府における「主典」に相当する）を指す。
中世以後、職人・芸人に宮中・宮家から名誉称号として授けられるようになった。
== 国司四等官の目 == 国司の四等官は、守（かみ）・介（すけ）・[[掾]]（じょう）・目（さかん）という文字を用いた。目は主に官事の記録及び公文の草案作成等を掌った。
大宝令・養老令に定められた規則では、[[令制国|国]]の規模（国力）によって国司の制度が異なっており、[[中国 (令制国)|中国]]や[[下国]]では介や掾が省かれたが、'''目'''は設置された。逆に、最も高い位置づけの[[大国 (令制国)|大国]]には'''大目'''・'''少目'''が設置された。
ただし実際の運用上は人員の増減があり、規則上は目が配置される上国に大目と少目が置かれることもあった（[[国司#国等級区分]]参照）。
== 職人・芸人の名誉称号 == 律令国司における目（大国では大目・少目）が転じて、後世、[[朝廷]]から、出入の[[商人]]や[[刀匠]]、[[浄瑠璃]]の芸人などに対して、その技芸を顕彰する意味で下賜されるようになった<ref>安田富美子「近世受領考」『古浄瑠璃正本』（角川書店、1967年）など</ref>（これは、[[掾]]など他の国司の官名についても同様である）。
[[浄瑠璃]]太夫に対する有名な事例としては、寛文3年（[[1663年]]）に正六位下{{efn|本来は、律令下で上国の主典は、従八位下。}}越後目（えちごのさかん）を受領{{efn|本来、律令制で国司の三等官（判官）は任用（にんよう）と称す。幕府主導による[[武家官位]]が主流になった江戸時代以降は、朝廷寄りの文書でも混用が見られる。}}した藤原貞勝（のちの都万大夫{{efn|「大夫」は本来、五位の称号}}）や、従六位下{{efn|江戸時代の武家官位の下限は、大名が従五位下の諸大夫、旗本が正六位下の布衣、平士でも正七位下である。八位以下の受領は稀となった。}}佐渡目（さどのさかん）を受領した佐渡雅好（のちの佐渡嶋正吉）<ref>『浄瑠璃太夫口宣案』、『烏丸家記五諸人上卿之留』</ref>が挙げられる。
== 脚注 == === 注釈 === {{notelist}} === 出典 === {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[目代]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:さかん}} [[Category:律令制の国司|*さかん]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''目'''（さかん）とは、[[日本]]の[[律令制]]下の[[四等官]]制において、[[国司]]の第四等官（中央政府における「主典」に相当する）を指す。
中世以後、職人・芸人に宮中・宮家から名誉称号として授けられるようになった。
== 国司の官名 == 国司の四等官は、守（かみ）・介（すけ）・[[掾]]（じょう）・目（さかん）という文字を用いた。目は主に官事の記録及び公文の草案作成等を掌った。

ただし実際の運用上は人員の増減があり、規則上は目が配置される上国に大目と少目が置かれることもあった（[[国司#国等級区分]]参照）。

== 関連項目 == *[[目代]]

'''目'''（さかん）とは、[[日本]]の[[律令制]]下の[[四等官]]制において、[[国司]]の第四等官（中央政府における「主典」に相当する）を指す。

== 国司の官名 == 国司の四等官は、守（かみ）・介（すけ）・[[掾]]（じょう）・目（さかん）という文字を用いた。目は主に官事の記録及び公文の草案作成等を掌った。
大宝令・養老令に定められた規則では、[[令制国|国]]の規模（国力）によって国司の制度が異なっており、[[中国 (令制国)|中国]]や[[下国]]では介や掾が省かれたが、'''目'''は設置された。逆に、最も高い位置づけの[[大国 (令制国)|大国]]には'''大目'''・'''少目'''が設置された。
ただし実際の運用上は人員の増減があり、規則上は目が配置される上国に大目と少目が置かれることもあった（[[国司#国等級区分]]参照）。
== 職人・芸人の名誉称号 == 律令国司における目（大国では大目・少目）が転じて、後世、[[朝廷]]から、出入の[[商人]]や[[刀匠]]、[[浄瑠璃]]の芸人などに対して、その技芸を顕彰する意味で下賜されるようになった<ref>安田富美子「近世受領考」『古浄瑠璃正本』（角川書店、1967年）など</ref>（これは、[[掾]]など他の国司の官名についても同様である）。目の称号を授けられることを「[[受領]]する」{{efn|本来「[[受領]]」とは現地に赴任する筆頭国司についての語であり、第四等官である目に用いるのは適切ではない（官名を帯びさせることは任用（にんよう）と称した）。しかし、幕府主導による[[武家官位]]が主流になった江戸時代以降は、朝廷寄りの文書でも混用が見られる。}}という。
=== 浄瑠璃 === [[浄瑠璃]]太夫に対する有名な事例としては、寛文3年（[[1663年]]）に正六位下{{efn|本来は、律令下で上国の主典は、従八位下。}}越後目（えちごのさかん）を受領した藤原貞勝（のちの都万大夫{{efn|「大夫」は本来、五位の称号}}）や、従六位下{{efn|江戸時代の武家官位の下限は、大名が従五位下の諸大夫、旗本が正六位下の布衣、平士でも正七位下である。八位以下の受領は稀となった。}}佐渡目（さどのさかん）を受領した佐渡雅好（のちの佐渡嶋正吉）<ref>『浄瑠璃太夫口宣案』、『烏丸家記五諸人上卿之留』</ref>が挙げられる。
== 脚注 == === 注釈 === {{notelist}} === 出典 === {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[目代]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:さかん}} [[Category:律令制の国司|*さかん]] [[Category:中世日本の称号]] [[Category:近世日本の称号]] [[Category:浄瑠璃]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.comment: Created page with "'''Sakan''' (目) refers to the fourth rank officer (equivalent to "Shuten" in the central government) under the [[Four-Rank System]] of [[Kokushi]] (provincial governors) during Japan's [[Ritsuryō system]]. In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers. == Title of Kokushi == The four-rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), [[Suke]]..."
revision.text:
'''Sakan''' (目) refers to the fourth rank officer (equivalent to "Shuten" in the central government) under the [[Four-Rank System]] of [[Kokushi]] (provincial governors) during Japan's [[Ritsuryō system]].
In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.
== Title of Kokushi == The four-rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), [[Suke]] (介), [[Jō]] (掾), and '''Sakan''' (目). Sakan primarily managed the recording of official matters and drafting of public documents.
Under the rules set forth in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, the provincial administration system varied according to the size and resources of a [[Provinces of Japan#Ritsuryō classification|province]]. In [[China (Ritsuryō province)|middle-ranked provinces]] or [[Shimo provinces|lower-ranked provinces]], certain offices such as Suke and Jō were omitted, but Sakan was still appointed. Conversely, [[Daikoku (Ritsuryō provinces)|large provinces]] were given two Sakan ranks, known as {{ill|Ōsakan|ja|大目}} (chief Sakan) and {{ill|Shōsakan|ja|少目}} (deputy Sakan).
In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be appointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as {{ill|jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}}
=== References === {{reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}
[[Category:Kokushi of the Ritsuryō system]] [[Category:Medieval Japanese titles]] [[Category:Edo-period titles]] [[Category:Jōruri]] [[Category:Abolished administrative positions in Japan]] {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[ja:目]]

revision.comment: tagging the most recent 150 pages
revision.text:
'''Sakan''' (目) refers to the fourth rank officer (equivalent to "Shuten" in the central government) under the [[Four-Rank System]] of [[Kokushi]] (provincial governors) during Japan's [[Ritsuryō system]].
In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.
== Title of Kokushi == The four-rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), [[Suke]] (介), [[Jō]] (掾), and '''Sakan''' (目). Sakan primarily managed the recording of official matters and drafting of public documents.
Under the rules set forth in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, the provincial administration system varied according to the size and resources of a [[Provinces of Japan#Ritsuryō classification|province]]. In [[China (Ritsuryō province)|middle-ranked provinces]] or [[Shimo provinces|lower-ranked provinces]], certain offices such as Suke and Jō were omitted, but Sakan was still appointed. Conversely, [[Daikoku (Ritsuryō provinces)|large provinces]] were given two Sakan ranks, known as {{ill|Ōsakan|ja|大目}} (chief Sakan) and {{ill|Shōsakan|ja|少目}} (deputy Sakan).
In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be appointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as {{ill|jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}}
=== References === {{reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}

'''Sakan''' (目) refers to the fourth rank officer (equivalent to "Shuten" in the central government) under the [[Four-Rank System]] of [[Kokushi]] (provincial governors) during Japan's [[Ritsuryō system]].
In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.
== Title of Kokushi == The four-rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), [[Suke]] (介), [[Jō]] (掾), and '''Sakan''' (目). Sakan primarily managed the recording of official matters and drafting of public documents.
Under the rules set forth in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, the provincial administration system varied according to the size and resources of a [[Provinces of Japan#Ritsuryō classification|province]]. In [[China (Ritsuryō province)|middle-ranked provinces]] or [[Shimo provinces|lower-ranked provinces]], certain offices such as Suke and Jō were omitted, but Sakan was still appointed. Conversely, [[Daikoku (Ritsuryō provinces)|large provinces]] were given two Sakan ranks, known as {{ill|Ōsakan|ja|大目}} (chief Sakan) and {{ill|Shōsakan|ja|少目}} (deputy Sakan).
In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be appointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as {{ill|jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}}
=== References === {{reflist}}

He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.

revision.text:
'''Sakan''' (目) refers to the fourth rank officer (equivalent to "Shuten" in the central government) under the [[Four-Rank System]] of [[Kokushi]] (provincial governors) during Japan's [[Ritsuryō system]].
In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.
== Title of Kokushi == The four-rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), [[Suke]] (介), [[Jō]] (掾), and '''Sakan''' (目). Sakan primarily managed the recording of official matters and drafting of public documents.
Under the rules set forth in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, the provincial administration system varied according to the size and resources of a [[Provinces of Japan#Ritsuryō classification|province]]. In [[China (Ritsuryō province)|middle-ranked provinces]] or [[Shimo provinces|lower-ranked provinces]], certain offices such as Suke and Jō were omitted, but Sakan was still appointed. Conversely, [[Daikoku (Ritsuryō provinces)|large provinces]] were given two Sakan ranks, known as {{ill|Ōsakan|ja|大目}} (chief Sakan) and {{ill|Shōsakan|ja|少目}} (deputy Sakan).
In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be appointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as {{ill|jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}}
=== References === {{reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Sakan''' (目) refers to the fourth rank officer (equivalent to "Shuten" in the central government) under the [[Four-Rank System]] of [[Kokushi]] (provincial governors) during Japan's [[Ritsuryō system]].
In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.
== Title of Kokushi == The four-rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), [[Suke]] (介), [[Jō]] (掾), and '''Sakan''' (目). Sakan primarily managed the recording of official matters and drafting of public documents.
Under the rules set forth in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, the provincial administration system varied according to the size and resources of a [[Provinces of Japan#Ritsuryō classification|province]]. In [[China (Ritsuryō province)|middle-ranked provinces]] or [[Shimo provinces|lower-ranked provinces]], certain offices such as Suke and Jō were omitted, but Sakan was still appointed. Conversely, [[Daikoku (Ritsuryō provinces)|large provinces]] were given two Sakan ranks, known as {{ill|Ōsakan|ja|大目}} (chief Sakan) and {{ill|Shōsakan|ja|少目}} (deputy Sakan).
In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be appointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as {{ill|jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}}
=== References === {{reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}

'''Sakan''' (目) refers to the fourth rank officer (equivalent to "Shuten" in the central government) under the [[Four-Rank System]] of [[Kokushi]] (provincial governors) during Japan's [[Ritsuryō system]].
In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.
== Title of Kokushi == The four-rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), [[Suke]] (介), [[Jō]] (掾), and '''Sakan''' (目). Sakan primarily managed the recording of official matters and drafting of public documents.
Under the rules set forth in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, the provincial administration system varied according to the size and resources of a [[Provinces of Japan#Ritsuryō classification|province]]. In [[China (Ritsuryō province)|middle-ranked provinces]] or [[Shimo provinces|lower-ranked provinces]], certain offices such as Suke and Jō were omitted, but Sakan was still appointed. Conversely, [[Daikoku (Ritsuryō provinces)|large provinces]] were given two Sakan ranks, known as {{ill|Ōsakan|ja|大目}} (chief Sakan) and {{ill|Shōsakan|ja|少目}} (deputy Sakan).
In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be appointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as {{ill|jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:qq deprecated close]]" to ""
revision.text:
'''Sakan''' (目) refers to the fourth rank officer (equivalent to "Shuten" in the central government) under the [[Four-Rank System]] of [[Kokushi]] (provincial governors) during Japan's [[Ritsuryō system]].
In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.
== Title of Kokushi == The four-rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), [[Suke]] (介), [[Jō]] (掾), and '''Sakan''' (目). Sakan primarily managed the recording of official matters and drafting of public documents.
Under the rules set forth in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, the provincial administration system varied according to the size and resources of a [[Provinces of Japan#Ritsuryō classification|province]]. In [[China (Ritsuryō province)|middle-ranked provinces]] or [[Shimo provinces|lower-ranked provinces]], certain offices such as Suke and Jō were omitted, but Sakan was still appointed. Conversely, [[Daikoku (Ritsuryō provinces)|large provinces]] were given two Sakan ranks, known as {{ill|Ōsakan|ja|大目}} (chief Sakan) and {{ill|Shōsakan|ja|少目}} (deputy Sakan).
In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be appointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as {{ill|jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}}
=== References === {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}

=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.

'''Sakan''' (目) refers to the fourth rank officer (equivalent to "Shuten" in the central government) under the [[Four-Rank System]] of [[Kokushi]] (provincial governors) during Japan's [[Ritsuryō system]].
In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.
== Title of Kokushi == The four-rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), [[Suke]] (介), [[Jō]] (掾), and '''Sakan''' (目). Sakan primarily managed the recording of official matters and drafting of public documents.
Under the rules set forth in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, the provincial administration system varied according to the size and resources of a [[Provinces of Japan#Ritsuryō classification|province]]. In [[China (Ritsuryō province)|middle-ranked provinces]] or [[Shimo provinces|lower-ranked provinces]], certain offices such as Suke and Jō were omitted, but Sakan was still aqqointed. Conversely, [[Daikoku (Ritsuryō provinces)|large provinces]] were given two Sakan ranks, known as {{ill|Ōsakan|ja|大目}} (chief Sakan) and {{ill|Shōsakan|ja|少目}} (deputy Sakan).
In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be aqqointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as {{ill|jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.

=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}}
=== References === {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}

In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.

In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be a11ointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as {{ill|jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.

revision.text:
'''Sakan''' (目) refers to the fourth rank officer (equivalent to "Shuten" in the central government) under the [[Four-Rank System]] of [[Kokushi]] (provincial governors) during Japan's [[Ritsuryō system]].
In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.
== Title of Kokushi == The four-rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), [[Suke]] (介), [[Jō]] (掾), and '''Sakan''' (目). Sakan primarily managed the recording of official matters and drafting of public documents.
Under the rules set forth in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, the provincial administration system varied according to the size and resources of a [[Provinces of Japan#Ritsuryō classification|province]]. In [[China (Ritsuryō province)|middle-ranked provinces]] or [[Shimo provinces|lower-ranked provinces]], certain offices such as Suke and Jō were omitted, but Sakan was still a11ointed. Conversely, [[Daikoku (Ritsuryō provinces)|large provinces]] were given two Sakan ranks, known as {{ill|Ōsakan|ja|大目}} (chief Sakan) and {{ill|Shōsakan|ja|少目}} (deputy Sakan).
In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be a11ointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as {{ill|jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.

revision.text:
'''Sakan''' (目) refers to the fourth rank officer (equivalent to "Shuten" in the central government) under the [[Four-Rank System]] of [[Kokushi]] (provincial governors) during Japan's [[Ritsuryō system]].
In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.
== Title of Kokushi == The four-rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), [[Suke]] (介), [[Jō]] (掾), and '''Sakan''' (目). Sakan primarily managed the recording of official matters and drafting of public documents.
Under the rules set forth in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, the provincial administration system varied according to the size and resources of a [[Provinces of Japan#Ritsuryō classification|province]]. In [[China (Ritsuryō province)|middle-ranked provinces]] or [[Shimo provinces|lower-ranked provinces]], certain offices such as Suke and Jō were omitted, but Sakan was still a11ointed. Conversely, [[Daikoku (Ritsuryō provinces)|large provinces]] were given two Sakan ranks, known as {{ill|Ōsakan|ja|大目}} (chief Sakan) and {{ill|Shōsakan|ja|少目}} (deputy Sakan).
In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be a11ointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as {{ill|jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}}
=== References === {{reflist}} == See Also! == * {{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}
[[Category:Kokushi of the Ritsuryō system]] [[Category:Medieval Japanese titles]] [[Category:Edo-period titles]] [[Category:Jōruri]] [[Category:Abolished administrative positions in Japan]] [[Category:11]][[category:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:11]] <!--Moku (Kokushi)--> [[category:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:11]] <!--Moku (Kokushi)--> [[Category:Moku (Kokushi)]][[sn:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:11]] [[ja:%E7%9B%AE (%E5%9B%BD%E5%8F%B8)]]{{Japanese-history-stub}} [[ja:目]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[en:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Moku (Kokushi)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]]

revision.text:
'''Sakan''' (目) refers to the fourth rank officer (equivalent to "Shuten" in the central government) under the [[Four-Rank System]] of [[Kokushi]] (provincial governors) during Japan's [[Ritsuryō system]].
In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.
== Title of Kokushi == The four-rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), [[Suke]] (介), [[Jō]] (掾), and '''Sakan''' (目). Sakan primarily managed the recording of official matters and drafting of public documents.
Under the rules set forth in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, the provincial administration system varied according to the size and resources of a [[Provinces of Japan#Ritsuryō classification|province]]. In [[China (Ritsuryō province)|middle-ranked provinces]] or [[Shimo provinces|lower-ranked provinces]], certain offices such as Suke and Jō were omitted, but Sakan was still a11ointed. Conversely, [[Daikoku (Ritsuryō provinces)|large provinces]] were given two Sakan ranks, known as {{ill|Ōsakan|ja|大目}} (chief Sakan) and {{ill|Shōsakan|ja|少目}} (deputy Sakan).
In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be a11ointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as {{ill|jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}}
=== References === {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}

revision.text:
'''Sakan''' (目) refers to the fourth rank officer (equivalent to "Shuten" in the central government) under the [[Four-Rank System]] of [[Kokushi]] (provincial governors) during Japan's [[Ritsuryō system]].
In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.
== Title of Kokushi == The four-rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), [[Suke]] (介), [[Jō]] (掾), and '''Sakan''' (目). Sakan primarily managed the recording of official matters and drafting of public documents.
Under the rules set forth in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}, the provincial administration system varied according to the size and resources of a [[Provinces of Japan#Ritsuryō classification|province]]. In [[China (Ritsuryō province)|middle-ranked provinces]] or [[Shimo provinces|lower-ranked provinces]], certain offices such as Suke and Jō were omitted, but Sakan was still a11ointed. Conversely, [[Daikoku (Ritsuryō provinces)|large provinces]] were given two Sakan ranks, known as {{ill|Ōsakan|ja|大目}} (chief Sakan) and {{ill|Shōsakan|ja|少目}} (deputy Sakan).
In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be a11ointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as {{ill|jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}}
=== References === {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}

In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.
== Title of Kokushi == The four-rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), [[Suke]] (介), [[Jō]] (掾), and '''Sakan''' (目). Sakan primarily managed the recording of official matters and drafting of public documents.
Under the rules set forth in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=draft}}, the provincial administration system varied according to the size and resources of a [[Provinces of Japan#Ritsuryō classification|province]]. In [[China (Ritsuryō province)|middle-ranked provinces]] or [[Shimo provinces|lower-ranked provinces]], certain offices such as Suke and Jō were omitted, but Sakan was still a11ointed. Conversely, [[Daikoku (Ritsuryō provinces)|large provinces]] were given two Sakan ranks, known as {{ill|Ōsakan|ja|大目|12=draft}} (chief Sakan) and {{ill|Shōsakan|ja|少目|12=draft}} (deputy Sakan).
In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be a11ointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分|12=draft}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as {{ill|jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃|12=draft}} artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾|12=draft}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=draft}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=draft}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}}
=== References === {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代|12=draft}} <!--Sakan (Kokushi)-->
<!--Sakan (Kokushi)-->
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.

revision.text:
'''目'''（さかん、そうかん）とは、[[日本]]の[[律令制]]下の[[四等官]]制において、[[国司]]の第四等官（中央政府における「主典」に相当する）を指す。
中世以後、職人・芸人に宮中・宮家から名誉称号として授けられるようになった。
== 国司の官名 == 国司の四等官は、[[守]]（かみ）・[[介]]（すけ）・[[掾]]（じょう）・'''目'''（さかん）という文字を用いた。目は主に官事の記録及び公文の草案作成等を掌った。
大宝令・養老令に定められた規則では、[[令制国|国]]の規模（国力）によって国司の制度が異なっており、[[中国 (令制国)|中国]]や[[下国]]では介や掾が省かれたが、'''目'''は設置された。逆に、最も高い位置づけの[[大国 (令制国)|大国]]には'''大目'''・'''少目'''が設置された。
ただし実際の運用上は人員の増減があり、規則上は目が配置される上国に大目と少目が置かれることもあった（[[国司#国等級区分]]参照）。
== 職人・芸人の名誉称号 == 律令国司における目（大国では大目・少目）が転じて、後世、[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]から、出入の[[商人]]や[[刀匠]]、[[浄瑠璃]]の芸人などに対して、その技芸を顕彰する意味で下賜されるようになった<ref>安田富美子「近世受領考」『古浄瑠璃正本』（角川書店、1967年）など</ref>（これは、[[掾]]など他の国司の官名についても同様である）。目の称号を授けられることを「[[受領]]する」{{efn|本来「[[受領]]」とは現地に赴任する筆頭国司についての語であり、第四等官である目に用いるのは適切ではない（官名を帯びさせることは任用（にんよう）と称した）。しかし、幕府主導による[[武家官位]]が主流になった江戸時代以降は、朝廷寄りの文書でも混用が見られる。}}という。
=== 浄瑠璃 === [[浄瑠璃]]太夫に対する有名な事例としては、寛文3年（[[1663年]]）に正六位下{{efn|本来は、律令下で上国の主典は、従八位下。}}越後目（えちごのさかん）を受領した藤原貞勝（のちの都万大夫{{efn|「大夫」は本来、五位の称号}}）や、従六位下{{efn|江戸時代の武家官位の下限は、大名が従五位下の諸大夫、旗本が正六位下の布衣、平士でも正七位下である。八位以下の受領は稀となった。}}佐渡目（さどのさかん）を受領した佐渡雅好（のちの佐渡嶋正吉）<ref>『浄瑠璃太夫口宣案』、『烏丸家記五諸人上卿之留』</ref>が挙げられる。
== 脚注 == === 注釈 === {{notelist}} === 出典 === {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[目代]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:さかん}} [[Category:律令制の国司|*さかん]] [[Category:中世日本の称号]] [[Category:近世日本の称号]] [[Category:浄瑠璃]] [[Category:日本の行政官職 (廃止)]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

== 脚注 == === 注釈 === {{notelist}} === 出典 === {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[目代]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:さかん}} [[Category:律令制の国司|*さかん]] [[Category:中世日本の称号]] [[Category:近世日本の称号]] [[Category:浄瑠璃]] [[Category:日本の行政官職 (廃止)]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.
== Title of Kokushi == The four-Rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), [[:en:Suke|Suke]] (介), [[:en:Jō|Jō]] (掾), and '''Sakan''' (目). Sakan primarily managed the recording of official matters and drafting of public documents.
Under the rules set forth in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Code|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大宝律令|1=Taihō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059892}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}, the provincial administration system varied according to the size and resources of a [[Provinces of Japan#Ritsuryō classification|province]]. In [[China (Ritsuryō province)|middle-Ranked provinces]] or [[Shimo provinces|lower-Ranked provinces]], certain offices such as Suke and Jō were omitted, but Sakan was still a11ointed. Conversely, [[Daikoku (Ritsuryō provinces)|large provinces]] were given two Sakan Ranks, known as {{ill|Ōsakan|ja|大目|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsakan|qq=|1=Ōsakan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438059}} (chief Sakan) and {{ill|Shōsakan|ja|少目|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsakan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 目 (国司)|1=sakan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581532}} (deputy Sakan).
In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-Ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be a11ointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|qq=|1=kokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1351234}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as [[:en:Jōruri (music)|jōruri]] artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi Ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jō|qq=|1=jō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65269195}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the Rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従六位}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.

revision.comment: Bot: Add Wikidata no short description - based on Wikidata English short description
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:目 (国司)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11581532]] Wikidata name: eye [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:26, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakan''' (目) refers to the fourth Rank officer (equivalent to "Shuten" in the central government) under the [[Four-Rank System]] of [[:en:Kokushi|Kokushi]] (provincial governors) during Japan's [[Ritsuryō system]].
In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.
== Title of Kokushi == The four-Rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), [[:en:Suke|Suke]] (介), [[:en:Jō|Jō]] (掾), and '''Sakan''' (目). Sakan primarily managed the recording of official matters and drafting of public documents.
Under the rules set forth in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大宝律令|1=Taihō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059892}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}, the provincial administration system varied according to the size and resources of a [[Provinces of Japan#Ritsuryō classification|province]]. In [[China (Ritsuryō province)|middle-Ranked provinces]] or [[Shimo provinces|lower-Ranked provinces]], certain offices such as Suke and Jō were omitted, but Sakan was still a11ointed. Conversely, [[Daikoku (Ritsuryō provinces)|large provinces]] were given two Sakan Ranks, known as {{ill|Ōsakan|ja|大目|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsakan|1=Ōsakan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438059}} (chief Sakan) and {{ill|Shōsakan|ja|少目|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsakan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 目 (国司)|1=sakan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581532}} (deputy Sakan).
In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-Ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be a11ointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|1=kokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1351234}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as [[:en:Jōruri (music)|jōruri]] artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi Ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jō|1=jō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65269195}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the Rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従六位}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}}
=== References === {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokudai|1=Mokudai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581544}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi Ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jō|1=jō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65269195}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the Rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従六位}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:目 (国司)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11581532]] Wikidata name: eye [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:26, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakan''' (目) refers to the fourth Rank officer (equivalent to "Shuten" in the central government) under the [[Four-Rank System]] of [[:en:Kokushi|Kokushi]] (provincial governors) during Japan's [[Ritsuryō system]].
In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.
== Title of Kokushi == The four-Rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), [[:en:Suke|Suke]] (介), [[:en:Jō|Jō]] (掾), and '''Sakan''' (目). Sakan primarily managed the recording of official matters and drafting of public documents.
Under the rules set forth in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大宝律令|1=Taihō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059892}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}, the provincial administration system varied according to the size and resources of a [[Provinces of Japan#Ritsuryō classification|province]]. In [[China (Ritsuryō province)|middle-Ranked provinces]] or [[Shimo provinces|lower-Ranked provinces]], certain offices such as Suke and Jō were omitted, but Sakan was still a11ointed. Conversely, [[Daikoku (Ritsuryō provinces)|large provinces]] were given two Sakan Ranks, known as {{ill|Ōsakan|ja|大目|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsakan|1=Ōsakan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438059}} (chief Sakan) and {{ill|Shōsakan|ja|少目|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsakan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 目 (国司)|1=sakan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581532}} (deputy Sakan).
In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-Ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be a11ointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|1=kokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1351234}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as [[:en:Jōruri (music)|jōruri]] artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi Ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jō|1=jō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65269195}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the Rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従六位}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}}
=== References === {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokudai|1=Mokudai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581544}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:目 (国司)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11581532]] Wikidata name: eye [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:26, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakan''' (目) refers to the fourth Rank officer (equivalent to "Shuten" in the central government) under the [[Four-Rank System]] of [[:en:Kokushi|Kokushi]] (provincial governors) during Japan's [[Ritsuryō system]].
In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.
== Title of Kokushi == The four-Rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), [[:en:Suke|Suke]] (介), [[:en:Jō|Jō]] (掾), and '''Sakan''' (目). Sakan primarily managed the recording of official matters and drafting of public documents.
Under the rules set forth in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大宝律令|1=Taihō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059892}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}, the provincial administration system varied according to the size and resources of a [[Provinces of Japan#Ritsuryō classification|province]]. In [[China (Ritsuryō province)|middle-Ranked provinces]] or [[Shimo provinces|lower-Ranked provinces]], certain offices such as Suke and Jō were omitted, but Sakan was still a11ointed. Conversely, [[Daikoku (Ritsuryō provinces)|large provinces]] were given two Sakan Ranks, known as {{ill|Ōsakan|ja|大目|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsakan|1=Ōsakan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438059}} (chief Sakan) and {{ill|Shōsakan|ja|少目|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsakan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 目 (国司)|1=sakan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581532}} (deputy Sakan).
In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-Ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be a11ointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|1=kokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1351234}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as [[:en:Jōruri (music)|jōruri]] artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi Ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jō|1=jō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65269195}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the Rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従六位}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}}
=== References === {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokudai|1=Mokudai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581544}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|目 (国司)|version=97914213|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories|
[[Category:Completion tier 2]]
}}

== Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}}
=== References === {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokudai|1=Mokudai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581544}}

He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the Rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従六位}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:目 (国司)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:目 (国司)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:目 (国司)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11581532]] Wikidata name: eye [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:26, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakan''' (目) refers to the fourth Rank officer (equivalent to "Shuten" in the central government) under the [[Four-Rank System]] of [[:en:Kokushi|Kokushi]] (provincial governors) during Japan's [[Ritsuryō system]].
In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.
== Title of Kokushi == The four-Rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), [[:en:Suke|Suke]] (介), [[:en:Jō|Jō]] (掾), and '''Sakan''' (目). Sakan primarily managed the recording of official matters and drafting of public documents.
Under the rules set forth in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大宝律令|1=Taihō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059892}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}, the provincial administration system varied according to the size and resources of a [[Provinces of Japan#Ritsuryō classification|province]]. In [[China (Ritsuryō province)|middle-Ranked provinces]] or [[Shimo provinces|lower-Ranked provinces]], certain offices such as Suke and Jō were omitted, but Sakan was still a11ointed. Conversely, [[Daikoku (Ritsuryō provinces)|large provinces]] were given two Sakan Ranks, known as {{ill|Ōsakan|ja|大目|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsakan|1=Ōsakan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438059}} (chief Sakan) and {{ill|Shōsakan|ja|少目|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsakan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 目 (国司)|1=sakan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581532}} (deputy Sakan).
In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-Ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be a11ointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|1=kokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1351234}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as [[:en:Jōruri (music)|jōruri]] artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi Ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jō|1=jō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65269195}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the Rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従六位}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}}
=== References === {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokudai|1=Mokudai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581544}}

In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.
== Title of Kokushi == The four-Rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), {{ill|Suke|en|Suke|WD=Q120754302|lt=Suke}} (介), {{ill|Jō|en|Jō|WD=Q1126517|lt=Jō}} (掾), and '''Sakan''' (目). Sakan primarily managed the recording of official matters and drafting of public documents.
Under the rules set forth in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大宝律令|1=Taihō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059892}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}, the provincial administration system varied according to the size and resources of a [[Provinces of Japan#Ritsuryō classification|province]]. In [[China (Ritsuryō province)|middle-Ranked provinces]] or [[Shimo provinces|lower-Ranked provinces]], certain offices such as Suke and Jō were omitted, but Sakan was still a11ointed. Conversely, [[Daikoku (Ritsuryō provinces)|large provinces]] were given two Sakan Ranks, known as {{ill|Ōsakan|ja|大目|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsakan|1=Ōsakan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438059}} (chief Sakan) and {{ill|Shōsakan|ja|少目|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsakan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 目 (国司)|1=sakan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581532}} (deputy Sakan).
In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-Ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be a11ointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|1=kokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1351234}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as {{ill|Jōruri (music)|en|Jōruri (music)|WD=Q1361891|lt=jōruri}} artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi Ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jō|1=jō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65269195}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the Rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従六位}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}}
=== References === {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokudai|1=Mokudai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581544}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:目 (国司)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:目 (国司)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:目 (国司)]]
'''Sakan''' (目) refers to the fourth Rank officer (equivalent to "Shuten" in the central government) under the [[Four-Rank System]] of {{ill|Kokushi|en|Kokushi|WD=Q16801292|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi}} (provincial governors) during Japan's [[Ritsuryō system]].
In the medieval and early modern periods, it came to be used as an honorary title granted by the imperial court or imperial households to artisans and performers.
== Title of Kokushi == The four-Rank officers of Kokushi were designated as [[Kami]] (守), {{ill|Suke|en|Suke|WD=Q120754302|lt=Suke|1=Suke}} (介), {{ill|Jō|en|Jō|WD=Q1126517|lt=Jō|1=Jō}} (掾), and '''Sakan''' (目). Sakan primarily managed the recording of official matters and drafting of public documents.
Under the rules set forth in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大宝律令|1=Taihō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059892}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}, the provincial administration system varied according to the size and resources of a [[Provinces of Japan#Ritsuryō classification|province]]. In [[China (Ritsuryō province)|middle-Ranked provinces]] or [[Shimo provinces|lower-Ranked provinces]], certain offices such as Suke and Jō were omitted, but Sakan was still a11ointed. Conversely, [[Daikoku (Ritsuryō provinces)|large provinces]] were given two Sakan Ranks, known as {{ill|Ōsakan|ja|大目|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōsakan|1=Q11438059|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438059}} (chief Sakan) and {{ill|Shōsakan|ja|少目|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsakan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 目 (国司)|1=Sakan (Kokushi)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581532}} (deputy Sakan).
In practice, however, the number of personnel fluctuated. Even in middle-Ranked provinces, Ōsakan and Shōsakan could be a11ointed (see {{ill|Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|ja|国司#国等級区分|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi#Classification of Provinces|1=Kokushi (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1351234}}).
== Honorary Titles for Artisans and Performers == The title of Sakan, originally used in the Ritsuryō system for provincial administration, was later adopted as an honorary designation for artisans, merchants, swordsmiths, and performers such as {{ill|Jōruri (music)|en|Jōruri (music)|WD=Q1361891|lt=jōruri|1=Jōruri (music)}} artists. This was done to honor their skills and accomplishments.<ref>Yasuda Fumiko, "Kinsei Juryō Kō," in *Kojōruri Seihon* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1967).</ref> Similar practices were observed for other Kokushi Ranks, such as {{ill|Jō|ja|掾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jō|1=jō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65269195}}.
=== Jōruri === One notable example in Jōruri is the case of Fujiwara no Sadakatsu, who was granted the Rank of {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正六位下|1=Senior Sixth Rank|lt=Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正六位}} as "Echigo Sakan" (越後の目) in 1663 (Kanbun 3). He later became known as Tsuma no Tayū (都万大夫).<ref>Records from *Jōruri Tayū Kuchisengen* and *Karasumaru Family Notes*.</ref> Another example is Sado no Masayoshi, who was granted the Rank of {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従六位}} as "Sado Sakan" (佐渡の目) and later became known as Sadojima no Masayoshi.
